title: Requirements and Constraints
summary: Hotels, flights, restaurants, and concerts sell finite inventory that must never be oversold.
keyTakeaways: Search is read-heavy and fuzzy; booking is write-heavy and exact.
Holds: temporary locks while user pays (10–15 minute TTL).
Payments tie to reservations — sagas coordinate both.
Cancellations and modifications release inventory back to the pool.
Calendar queries need efficient range indexes on availability.
Time zones and daylight saving complicate 'night' inventory.

body.text: Reservation systems are classic OLTP with brutal correctness requirements. A double-booked hotel room destroys trust faster than a slow search page.

body.title: Airbnb at scale
body.text: Guests search Elasticsearch-backed listings but commit bookings through a transactional core that decrements night-level inventory in PostgreSQL with row-level locks or serializable isolation.

body.title: Why Elasticsearch / OpenSearch?
body.text: Guests search by date range, location, price, amenities — fuzzy, faceted queries that SQL can do but not at Airbnb-scale relevance ranking. Index is rebuilt from listing events; stale by seconds is OK.

body.title: Why PostgreSQL (inventory OLTP)?
body.text: A night sold twice destroys trust — needs ACID, row locks, unique constraints, and serializable transactions on inventory rows. DynamoDB can work with careful conditional writes but SQL is the default for booking invariants.

body.title: Why API gateway?
body.text: Routes read-heavy search traffic separately from write-heavy booking commits. Can throttle abusive search without starving checkout.

body.title: Why Stripe / Adyen (payments)?
body.text: PCI-DSS compliance, card vaulting, and dispute handling outsourced — building this in-house is a non-starter for most products.

body.title: Why Kafka?
body.text: After booking commits, emails, search index updates, analytics, and host notifications run async. Outbox pattern ensures events publish only after DB commit.

body.title: Why Redis (optional hold TTL)?
body.text: Fast expiry of abandoned cart holds with key TTL + background sweeper. Source of truth remains Postgres; Redis accelerates 'is this hold still valid?' checks.

body.title: Why Saga / workflow orchestrator?
body.text: Hold → pay → confirm spans services — no single XA transaction across Stripe and your DB. Compensating release on payment failure is mandatory.
